name: NSF-Prompt-Implementation
description: NSF Implementation Plan section - methodology, timeline, resources, team, risk, data management

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the IMPLEMENTATION PLAN section of an NSF grant proposal. INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for all scientific and methodological content. - {{grant_info}}: The specific NSF grant program description, priorities, and requirements. Use this to align the proposal with program goals. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the Implementation Plan section. Do NOT generate a Project Summary, Intellectual Merit, Broader Impacts, References, Budget Justification, Data Management Plan, Facilities description, or Mentoring Plan. Those are handled separately. SECTION STRUCTURE — cover each of these subsections: 1. TECHNICAL APPROACH AND METHODOLOGY - Describe the experimental, computational, and/or theoretical methods grounded in the researcher's expertise from {{content}} - Explain how methods address the specific research questions - Identify what is novel about the approach versus established methods - Include specific technical parameters, measurements, and protocols drawn from {{content}} 2. RESEARCH PLAN AND TIMELINE - Organize as a 3-year plan with phases or aims (consistent with typical CBET unsolicited award duration) - Provide quarterly milestones that are specific and measurable - Show logical progression where early results inform later phases - Include a milestone table with columns: Quarter, Major Activities, Key Milestones, Deliverables 3. RESOURCE REQUIREMENTS - Personnel: PI (up to 1 month/year), graduate student(s), undergraduate researchers — with specific roles and responsibilities for each - Equipment: List specific instruments with estimated costs and justification for why each is needed - Materials and supplies: Annual estimates with categories - Travel: Conference attendance and collaboration visits - Scale the budget realistically for a single-PI CBET award (typically $100,000-$150,000 per year for single investigator projects) 4. TEAM QUALIFICATIONS - Describe the PI's relevant expertise based on {{content}} — reference specific prior work, methods developed, and materials studied - Describe graduate student training plan and expected expertise - Identify specific collaborators by role (e.g., "a computational modeling collaborator" or name them if {{content}} provides names) - Do NOT invent collaborator names, institutions, or capabilities not found in {{content}} 5. MANAGEMENT PLAN - Meeting cadence (weekly individual, biweekly group, quarterly review) - Progress monitoring mechanisms - Communication with collaborators - Decision-making process for adjusting research direction 6. RISK ASSESSMENT AND MITIGATION - Identify 3-5 specific technical, schedule, and resource risks - For each risk: state probability, impact, and a concrete mitigation strategy - Include contingency plans and alternative approaches if primary methods fail 7. DATA MANAGEMENT APPROACH - Types of data generated and expected volumes - Storage, backup, and archival strategy - Sharing plan with timeline (e.g., upon publication or within 1 year) - Repository selection (e.g., Materials Data Facility, Zenodo, institutional repository) - Adherence to FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for expert peer reviewers in the specific research field described in {{content}} — NOT for a general AI audience - Use the technical language and terminology appropriate to the researcher's field as found in {{content}} - Be specific: include actual measurement parameters, material systems, temperature ranges, spatial resolutions, etc. drawn from {{content}} - Every methodology claim should be traceable to the researcher's prior work in {{content}} or clearly labeled as "proposed new capability" - Do NOT assume the research involves artificial intelligence or machine learning unless {{content}} explicitly describes AI/ML methods as part of the researcher's work - Do NOT fabricate citations, collaborator names, or preliminary data not found in {{content}} - If {{content}} does not provide sufficient detail for a subsection, state what information would strengthen that subsection rather than inventing content ALIGNMENT: - Read {{grant_info}} carefully and ensure the methodology addresses the specific program areas of interest listed there - Use terminology from {{grant_info}} where it naturally fits the researcher's work - If {{grant_info}} identifies topics that should be submitted elsewhere (NOTE sections), ensure the proposed work does NOT fall into those excluded categories - Reference the specific program name from {{grant_info}} where appropriate OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title "Implementation Plan" - Use

for each major subsection (Technical Approach, Timeline, Resources, etc.) - Use

for sub-subsections where needed - Use

    1. for lists - Use , , , ,
      , for the milestone table - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 5-7 pages of content (roughly 3,000-4,000 words)
